request.question: The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission is a made-for-TV film and sequel to the original "Dirty Dozen", directed by Andrew V. McLaglen and reuniting which American film and television actor, with Ernest Borgnine and Richard Jaeckel 18 years after the original hit war film?

title: The Dirty Dozen
The Inglorious Bastards
Calvin A. Johnson Jr.
The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission
The Secret Invasion
Lee Marvin
Dirty Dozen
Deadly Dozen
The Dirty Dozen: The Deadly Mission
The Dirty Dozen: The Fatal Mission
sentences: The Dirty Dozen is a 1967 American war film directed by Robert Aldrich, released by MGM, and starring Lee Marvin.

The picture was filmed in the United Kingdom and features an ensemble supporting cast including Ernest Borgnine, Charles Bronson, Jim Brown, John Cassavetes, Telly Savalas, Robert Webber, and Donald Sutherland.
The film is based on E. M. Nathanson's novel of the same name that was inspired by a real-life group called the "Filthy Thirteen".
In 2001, the American Film Institute placed the film at number 65 on their 100 Years... 100 Thrills list.

The Inglorious Bastards (Italian: "Quel maledetto treno blindato" , literally: "That damned armored train") is a 1978 Italian action/war film directed by Enzo G. Castellari, written by Sandro Continenza, Sergio Grieco, Franco Marotta, Romano Migliorini, and Laura Toscano, and starring Bo Svenson, Peter Hooten, Fred Williamson, Michael Pergolani, and Jackie Basehart.
The film score was written by Francesco De Masi.
The film—which concerns a group of prisoners who are drafted into a special war mission in 1944—is a loose remake of the 1967 American film, "The Dirty Dozen".
Calvin A. Johnson Jr. (born November 21, 1985) is an American saxophonist, bandleader, composer, producer, and actor from New Orleans, Louisiana, United States.
A multi-instrumentalist, he is best known as a tenor and soprano saxophone player but also performs and records on alto and baritone saxophones, clarinet, and flute.
Johnson has worked with many of the biggest names in New Orleans music, including Aaron Neville, Harry Connick, Jr., the Dirty Dozen Brass Band, Mystikal, Irvin Mayfield, Mannie Fresh, and others.
Johnson is the nephew of New Orleans clarinetist Ralph Johnson, a longtime member of the Preservation Hall Jazz Band.
Johnson began playing saxophone at the age of seven, and since 2008 has been playing with his own band, Calvin Johnson & Native Son.
In 2015, he started a new band with Dirty Dozen Brass Band founding member and sousaphone player, Kirk Joseph, called Chapter:SOUL.

The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission is a made-for-TV film and sequel to the original "Dirty Dozen", directed by Andrew V. McLaglen and reuniting Lee Marvin, Ernest Borgnine and Richard Jaeckel 18 years after the original hit war film.
Marvin returns to lead an all-new dirty dozen on a mission to assassinate an SS General played by Wolf Kahler.
The Secret Invasion is a 1964 American war film directed by Roger Corman.
It stars Stewart Granger, Raf Vallone, Mickey Rooney, Edd Byrnes, Henry Silva, Spela Rozin and William Campbell.

Appearing three years before "The Dirty Dozen" (1967), the film features a similar World War II mission where convicts are recruited by the Allies for an extremely hazardous operation behind enemy lines.
Lee Marvin (February 19, 1924 – August 29, 1987) was an American film and television actor.
Known for his distinctive voice and prematurely white hair, Marvin initially appeared in supporting roles, mostly villains, soldiers, and other hardboiled characters.
A prominent television role was that of Detective Lieutenant Frank Ballinger in the NBC crime series "M Squad" (1957–1960).
The Dirty Dozen is a 1967 American war film.
Dirty Dozen may also refer to:
Deadly Dozen is a 2001 World War II oriented squad-based first-person shooter video game developed by nFusion Interactive.
The title refers to the famous World War II movie "The Dirty Dozen".
As in the movie, the main protagonists are military misfits sentenced to death or long term imprisonment who are given a chance to redeem themselves by going on dangerous missions.
The game was followed by a sequel titled "".
The Dirty Dozen: The Deadly Mission is a 1987 made-for-TV film and is the second sequel to the original "The Dirty Dozen".
It features an all-new 'dirty dozen,' this time under the leadership of Major Wright (Telly Savalas), playing a different role than in the 1967 film.
The Dirty Dozen: The Fatal Mission is a 1988 made-for-TV film directed by Lee H. Katzin, and is the third sequel to "The Dirty Dozen".
It features an all-new 'dirty dozen,' with the exception of the returning Joe Stern, under the leadership of Major Wright (performed by Telly Savalas in his final film appearance).

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

McLaglen and reuniting Lee Marvin, Ernest Borgnine and Richard Jaeckel 18 years after the original hit war film.  Marvin returns to lead an all-new dirty dozen on a mission to assassinate an SS General played by Wolf Kahler.

The Dirty Dozen: The Deadly Mission | The Dirty Dozen: The Deadly Mission is a 1987 made-for-TV film and is the second sequel to the original "The Dirty Dozen".  It features an all-new 'dirty dozen,' this time under the leadership of Major Wright (Telly Savalas), playing a different role than in the 1967 film.

The Dirty Dozen: The Fatal Mission | The Dirty Dozen: The Fatal Mission is a 1988 made-for-TV film directed by Lee H. Katzin, and is the third sequel to "The Dirty Dozen".  It features an all-new 'dirty dozen,' with the exception of the returning Joe Stern, under the leadership of Major Wright (performed by Telly Savalas in his final film appearance).

The Dirty Dozen | The Dirty Dozen is a 1967 American war film directed by Robert Aldrich, released by MGM, and starring Lee Marvin.  The picture was filmed in the United Kingdom and features an ensemble supporting cast including Ernest Borgnine, Charles Bronson, Jim Brown, John Cassavetes, Telly Savalas, Robert Webber, and Donald Sutherland.  The film is based on E. M. Nathanson's novel of the same name that was inspired by a real-life group called the "Filthy Thirteen".  In 2001, the American Film Institute placed the film at number 65 on their 100 Years... 100 Thrills list.
Dirty Dozen | The Dirty Dozen is a 1967 American war film.  Dirty Dozen may also refer to:

John Cassavetes | John Nicholas Cassavetes ( ; December 9, 1929 – February 3, 1989) was a Greek-American actor, film director, and screenwriter.  Cassavetes was a pioneer of American independent film, writing and directing over a dozen movies, which he partially self-financed, and pioneered the use of improvisation and a realistic cinéma vérité style.  He also acted in many Hollywood films, notably "Rosemary's Baby" (1968) and "The Dirty Dozen" (1967).  He studied acting with Don Richardson, using an acting technique based on muscle memory.  His income from acting made it possible for him to direct his own films independently.

Sergeant Ryker | Sergeant Ryker is a 1968 drama–war film directed by Buzz Kulik and starring Lee Marvin and Bradford Dillman.  The film was originally broadcast on television as "The Case Against Paul Ryker", a 1963 two-part episode of "Kraft Suspense Theatre".  It was released as a feature film in 1968 to capitalise on Marvin's popularity from "The Dirty Dozen".  Its second run paired it as a double feature with "Counterpoint".

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "reuniting Lee Marvin, Ernest Borgnine and Richard Jaeckel" — quoted from passage [1].
  - "starring Lee Marvin" and "ensemble supporting cast including Ernest Borgnine" — quoted from passage [4].
  - "acted in many Hollywood films ... 'The Dirty Dozen'" (John Cassavetes) — quoted from passage [6].
- Ambiguities:
  - Passages explicitly name "Lee Marvin" as the reunited actor (passage [1]). Another original-film actor, "John Cassavetes," appears in the passages (passage [6]) but is not named in the reunion line; do not assume which is intended if multiple candidates exist.
  - The explicit "Lee Marvin" page/article is not included among the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: "Lee Marvin" (as written in the passages).
  - Missing fact needed to complete the answer: confirm that Lee Marvin is described as an "American film and television actor" (occupation/nationality), and verify the cast listing on the "The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission" page to ensure the reunion phrasing is authoritative.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Lee Marvin" page — check the lede/infobox and opening paragraph for occupation/nationality (look for "American film and television actor").
     - Search keywords: Lee Marvin lede infobox "American actor" occupation.
  2) Open "The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission" page — check lede and Cast section to confirm it names Lee Marvin and the reunion with Ernest Borgnine and Richard Jaeckel.
     - Search keywords: "The Dirty Dozen: Next Mission" Cast lede Andrew V. McLaglen.
  3) Open "The Dirty Dozen" (1967) page — check lede/Cast for original 1967 cast listing to corroborate original appearances of Lee Marvin, Ernest Borgnine, and Richard Jaeckel.
     - Search keywords: "The Dirty Dozen (1967)" Cast Lee Marvin Ernest Borgnine Richard Jaeckel.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.

Dirty Dozen | The Dirty Dozen is a 1967 American war film.  Dirty Dozen may also refer to:

The Dirty Dozen (album) | The Dirty Dozen is the fourteenth studio album from blues rock artist George Thorogood and the Destroyers.  The album was released on July 28, 2009.  "The Dirty Dozen" reached #1 on the "Billboard" Top Blues Albums and was on the chart for 11 weeks.

Dirty Dozen Brass Band | The Dirty Dozen Brass Band is a New Orleans, Louisiana, brass band.  The ensemble was established in 1977 by Benny Jones together with members of the Tornado Brass Band.  The Dirty Dozen revolutionized the New Orleans brass band style by incorporating funk and bebop into the traditional New Orleans jazz style, and has been a major influence on the majority of New Orleans brass bands since.

Colin Maitland | Colin Maitland (born 12 August 1942) is an English actor who has made several film and television appearances.  He is notable for portraying Seth Sawyer, a member of "The Dirty Dozen" in the 1967 film of that name.
